title: Benjamini–Tzalik Conjecture on connectivity and reconstructibility
statement_text: There exists a connectivity threshold $k_0$ such that every $k_0$-connected graph embedded in $\mathbb{R}$ has its full vertex set reconstructible from the edge lengths.
context_text: It is folklore that a graph is globally rigid in $\mathbb{R}$ if and only if it is 2-connected. Benjamini and Tzalik conjectured an analogous connectivity condition would guarantee reconstructibility for arbitrary point sets in $\mathbb{R}$. The current paper disproves this via Theorem 1.5.
paper_contribution: Disproves the conjecture via Theorem 1.5: for every positive integer $k$, there exist $k$-connected graphs on arbitrarily many vertices embeddable in the real line such that the largest reconstructible subset of vertices has size exactly $k$.
notes: The exact statement of the Benjamini–Tzalik conjecture is not quoted verbatim in the paper; reconstructed from context. PDF source — math may be garbled.
paper_title: Reconstructing a point set from a random subset of its pairwise distances

title: Informal conjecture on sharp threshold for linear-proportion reconstruction
statement_text: The correct threshold for the existence of a reconstructible set of size $\Omega(n)$ is $p = (1+\varepsilon)/n$.
context_text: Theorem 1.2a is proved with constant 42, i.e., $p \geq 42/n$ suffices. The authors note in footnote 2 that the best their methods could give is $9/n$, and they believe the true threshold is $(1+\varepsilon)/n$, with further discussion deferred to Section 4 on open problems.
notes: Appears in footnote 2 as an informal belief. Section 4 (open problems) is not present in the extracted text, so additional conjectures from that section may be missing.
paper_title: Reconstructing a point set from a random subset of its pairwise distances
